fingerprint: The claimed 0–0.5 arcsec range is borrowed from globe-derived observations with no dome mechanism to produce variation
globe_value: Parallax = 1 AU / distance — produces a continuous distribution from 0.769" (Proxima Cen) to <0.001" (distant stars), with the range 0–0.5" covering stars between ~2 pc and infinity
dome_value: 20m wobble at H = 9,086 km produces exactly arctan(20/9,086,000) = 0.454" — a single fixed value, not a range
match_precision: order-of-magnitude (0.454" falls within the 0–0.77" observed range but is not a precise constant match)

analysis.parametric_fingerprint: Weak. Unlike WIN-001 (1.57c = π/2) or the coordinate system (a = πR_Earth), there is no clean algebraic equation where a recognized globe constant appears in the dome's parallax formula. The 20m wobble amplitude is a free parameter fitted to produce the right order of magnitude, not derived from dome geometry or any globe-borrowed value.
analysis.structural_fingerprint: Moderate. The dome claims parallax falls in the range '0–0.5 arcsec', but this range has no derivation from dome physics. A rigid firmament wobble of fixed amplitude at fixed height produces exactly one angular shift: 0.454 arcsec. The dome writes '0–0.5' because that is the range observed by globe-based astrometry for nearby stars (within ~2 parsecs). The range itself is a globe fingerprint — the dome is quoting the globe's distance-dependent prediction as if it were its own, without any mechanism to produce the variation within the range.
analysis.measurement_infrastructure: The parallax values the dome's claim is calibrated against (Proxima 0.768", Sirius 0.379", etc.) are themselves computed by Hipparcos/Gaia using: (1) the ICRS coordinate frame (geocentric, assuming spherical Earth), (2) a baseline of 2 AU (Earth's orbital diameter), (3) reduction pipelines that model heliocentric aberration. The dome takes outputs of globe-assuming instruments and declares them consistent with firmament wobble, without acknowledging that the measurement infrastructure presupposes the geometry it claims to replace.
analysis.reverse_engineering_chain: The 20m amplitude is fully determined by the target: if you want ~0.45" at H = 9,086 km, you need d = H × tan(0.45/206265) = 20.0 m. The dome model provides no independent derivation of why the firmament should wobble by exactly 20 meters — the amplitude is chosen to reproduce the observation. This is the post-hoc fitting pattern identified in code_analysis (post_hoc: true, derives_from_dome: false).

why_it_matters: While the parametric fingerprint is weak (no clean constant match), the structural fingerprint reveals a deeper problem: the dome cannot explain why different stars show different parallaxes. On the globe, parallax variation is an automatic consequence of distance diversity plus a fixed baseline. On the dome, all stars are at the same height, so a fixed wobble gives a fixed shift. The '0–0.5 arcsec range' is borrowed from globe observations with no dome mechanism to produce it. To match the observed continuous 1/d distribution, the dome would need each star's wobble to be individually tuned — which is unfalsifiable curve-fitting, not prediction.
suggested_text: The dome's claimed range of 0–0.5 arcsec for parallax has no derivation from dome physics: a 20m wobble at 9,086 km produces exactly 0.454 arcsec for every star, not a range. The variation within the range exists only in globe-based observations, where it arises from distance diversity — the dome borrows the globe's output distribution while lacking any mechanism to generate it.
notes: Classified as structural_borrowing rather than parametric_match. The fingerprint is real but weaker than the strong cases (WIN-001 π/2, Section 4.5.9 πR). Most of the argumentative weight here is already captured in our existing Refuted by Data rebuttal. The fingerprint adds nuance (the range claim is specifically borrowed) but doesn't constitute a new kill-shot.
